story:
There are three rules in the house. Ben recites them every night. Auggie hums them like a song. Rule One: Knock three times on the wall. Rule Two: Whisper your name, so the house knows who to protect. Rule Three: Leave the milk by the door. Always.

I don’t remember when the ritual started. It just… was.

I don’t believe in monsters. I believe in quiet. In rituals that keep kids feeling safe.

Then the milk started disappearing.

One morning Auggie’s cup was empty. She hadn’t touched it. Lulu, our dog, had slept crated, and she doesn’t lick anything that clean. The next night, the milk was gone again. The hall door stood open. The air felt drained. I thought I smelled wet fur. Cinnamon. Lulu growled. That was new.

“Dad?” Ben said, chalk pale. “There’s someone outside my window.” On that side of the house there’s no tree. Just fence. And the woods. The kind sunlight avoids.

That night our ritual faltered. Ben’s voice cracked on his name. Auggie dropped her cup; the spill looked like a wound. Then, from the hall: three soft knocks. Our knocks. The same gentle rhythm, too polite to be kind.

A hinge-slow creak. A voice like distance pretending. “Audrey…” The name wrinkled wrong. Then sharper: “bEn.” Teeth in the whisper. I rushed the kids to bed. Read fast. Behind their closed doors, another name slid out, too close to my ear. “daNIel…”

I lay on the couch and waited. At 2:37 a.m., the milk didn’t fall. It tipped. A shadow pooled by the door, less still than stillness should be. I whispered, “Who’s there?” The dark whispered back, almost right: “Who’s there?” I turned on every light.

Morning tasted like metal. Ben wouldn’t drink. Lulu wouldn’t go near the door. In the wood by the lock: three shallow dents. Not scratches. Knocks set into the grain. When I pressed my ear to the door, I heard silence pretending it wasn’t hungry.

I texted my mother. “Hey, random question. Did we do that milk thing when I was a kid?” An hour later: “Stop doing it.” When she finally answered my call, her voice had another voice braided through it. A beat behind.

Three more knocks. Soft. Patient. Then, clear as a practiced mouth: “Ben. Auggie. Daddy.” I yanked the door open. Empty hall. Rain and old pennies in the air. The milk was gone again.

I tried to keep the day normal. Pretending is most of parenting. But that night Ben refused the ritual. “It’s not protecting us,” he said. “It’s feeding it.” I told him it was just superstition. He stared at the glass. The rim was scuffed like something had been testing teeth.

I dreamed the woods. Trees bowed away from a center that felt like a held breath. Something unfolded wearing my face, and my father’s, and faces older than firelight. Cold touched my outstretched hand. The world tilted wrong.

I woke to cinnamon and wet fur. The hall light was off. Footprints tracked the floor. Small. Bare. Wrong. Too many toes. One set went out. One came back in. Beside the door, written in milk-white: WHO DO I PROTECT NOW?

The smell lingered. Burnt sugar. Old fur. The kids’ beds were empty. Their window was open to the black line of trees. From the woods came three knocks. Knock. Knock. Knock. Our rhythm. Our rules, learned so well something else could use them.
